sourceLabel: USCIS — Form N-400, Application for Naturalization
notes: Dated facts for the /tools/citizenship-checklist tool (read by src/lib/citizenship.ts). Every value flagged TODO(owner) must be re-verified against uscis.gov before relying on it — naturalization fees, the civics-test rule, and vetting policy change. See docs/DATA-UPDATE-PLAYBOOK.md.

fees._fee_note: 2026 N-400 filing fee. TODO(owner): verify against the current USCIS fee schedule (uscis.gov/g-1055) — fees changed in the April 2024 rule and can change again.
fees.waiverNote: Form I-912 (full fee waiver) and Form I-942 (reduced fee) exist for applicants who qualify by income or hardship. TODO(owner): confirm I-942 income bands still apply under the current fee rule.

civicsTest._cutoff_note: Applicants who FILED N-400 on or after this date take the 2025 civics test; those who filed before take the 2008 test. TODO(owner): verify the cutoff date and rules at uscis.gov/citizenship/find-study-materials-and-resources/study-for-the-test before launch.
civicsTest.englishTest: The English test is unchanged: a Speaking test (during the interview), a Reading test (read 1 of up to 3 sentences correctly), and a Writing test (write 1 of up to 3 sentences correctly).
civicsTest.exemptions: Age/residence English exemptions may apply (e.g. 50/20 and 55/15 rules), and a 65/20 applicant gets a smaller civics list. TODO(owner): confirm exemption rules.

bases.label: 5-year permanent resident
bases.description: Standard path: green card held for 5 years.

bases.label: 3-year spouse of a US citizen
bases.description: Married to and living with the same US citizen for the last 3 years, and your spouse has been a citizen that whole time.

bases.label: Military service member / veteran
bases.description: Special rules under INA 328/329 can reduce or waive the residence and physical-presence requirements.
bases.note: Military naturalization (peacetime INA 328 or wartime INA 329) has its own rules and Form N-426. TODO(owner): this tool gives only general pointers for the military basis — verify specifics with the military JAG / USCIS military line.

requirements._residence_note: A single trip abroad of more than 6 months (~183 days) creates a rebuttable presumption that continuous residence was broken; a trip of 1 year or more breaks it automatically (absent an approved N-470). TODO(owner): verify day thresholds and N-470 exception at uscis.gov.

painPoints.title: A harder civics test for 2025+ filers
painPoints.summary: If you filed (or will file) your N-400 on or after October 20, 2025, you take the new 2025 civics test: 128-question study pool, 20 questions asked at the interview, and you must answer 12 correctly to pass. Filed before that date? You keep the 2008 test (100-question pool, 10 asked, 6 to pass).
painPoints.whyNow: The pass bar roughly doubled. Knowing which test applies to you decides how much you need to study — this tool derives it from your filing date.

painPoints.title: Expanded background checks and a holistic character review
painPoints.summary: USCIS has restored more thorough vetting in 2025–26: revived in-person neighborhood investigations, fuller background checks, and a holistic Good Moral Character review that weighs criminal history (any arrest, even dismissed), full tax compliance, child support, Selective Service registration, and review of your PUBLIC social media.
painPoints.whyNow: Decisions increasingly turn on the whole picture, not just a checklist. Being able to explain your record calmly and consistently matters more than ever.

painPoints.title: Hiding an arrest or tax issue is the #1 self-inflicted denial
painPoints.summary: USCIS pulls your FBI fingerprint record and IRS data. Failing to disclose an arrest, citation, or tax problem — even a dismissed charge from years ago — looks like a lack of good moral character and is a leading cause of denial. Disclose everything and bring certified court dispositions for every incident.
painPoints.whyNow: The cover-up is treated as worse than the underlying issue. Full disclosure with documents is almost always the safer path.

painPoints.title: Filing US taxes as a 'non-resident' after getting your green card
painPoints.summary: Green card holders are US tax residents and generally must file as residents on worldwide income. Filing a US non-resident return (or claiming to be a non-resident for tax treaty benefits) after becoming an LPR — while telling USCIS you've kept US residence — is treated as a near-fatal inconsistency that can suggest abandonment of residence or lack of good moral character.
painPoints.whyNow: This is a common, avoidable trap for NRIs who keep India ties. If this may be your situation, talk to a cross-border tax pro and an immigration attorney before filing N-400.

painPoints.title: Long trips to India can break your residency clock
painPoints.summary: A single trip abroad of 6+ months raises a rebuttable presumption that you broke continuous residence; a trip of 1 year or more breaks it automatically. Build a complete, accurate travel log (exact entry/exit dates for every trip) before you file — the interview will test it.
painPoints.whyNow: NRIs visit India often and for long stretches. One overlooked long trip can reset your eligibility date or sink the application.

painPoints.title: Two or more DUIs in the period usually means denial
painPoints.summary: Under current USCIS interpretation, two or more DUI convictions during the statutory good-moral-character period (5 or 3 years) create a strong presumption against good moral character and very often lead to denial. A single old DUI is usually explainable with the right documents — disclose it either way.
painPoints.whyNow: DUI handling has tightened. If this applies to you, get certified dispositions and legal advice before filing.

painPoints.title: Naturalizing means giving up Indian citizenship
painPoints.summary: India does not allow dual citizenship. Becoming a US citizen automatically ends your Indian citizenship — you must surrender your Indian passport and obtain a 'Surrender Certificate', then you can apply for an OCI (Overseas Citizen of India) card to keep lifelong visa-free travel and most rights (except voting, government jobs, and farmland purchase).
painPoints.whyNow: This is a post-oath task many new citizens forget; travelling to India on a cancelled Indian passport causes problems. Plan the OCI step in advance.

items.label: Residency clock met (or within 90 days of it)
items.detail: You can file up to 90 days before your 5-year (or 3-year) green-card anniversary. The tool computes your earliest filing date above.

items.label: Physical-presence requirement met
items.detail: Physically present in the US for at least 30 months of the last 5 years (or 18 months of the last 3 years for the spouse track).

items.label: Lived in your current state/USCIS district 3+ months
items.detail: Students may file where they live or where their parents live.

items.label: No single trip abroad that broke continuous residence
items.detail: Review every trip of 6+ months. A trip of 1 year+ breaks continuous residence automatically.

items.label: Green card (Permanent Resident Card), front and back
Current passport AND all expired passports
items.detail: Expired passports help reconstruct your exact travel history — USCIS may ask for stamps.

items.label: State ID / driver's license
Complete written travel history with exact entry/exit dates
items.detail: Every trip outside the US since you became an LPR (or last 5/3 years). Pull dates from passport stamps, airline emails, and your I-94 travel history.

items.label: IRS tax return TRANSCRIPTS for the last 5 years (3 for spouse track)
items.detail: Order tax transcripts free from the IRS (irs.gov 'Get Transcript') — bring these rather than just your own copies.

items.label: Certified court dispositions for ANY arrest, citation, or charge — even if dismissed
items.detail: Get the certified final disposition from the court for every incident, including dismissals and expunged records. Disclose everything.

items.label: Selective Service registration confirmation
items.detail: Males who lived in the US between ages 18 and 25 should confirm registration at sss.gov and print the confirmation.

items.label: Marriage certificate + proof you live together + spouse's proof of US citizenship
items.detail: 3-year spouse track only: spouse's birth certificate / naturalization certificate / US passport, plus joint lease, accounts, taxes.

items.label: Name-change documents (if your name differs across documents)
items.detail: Marriage certificate, divorce decree, or court order linking your names.

items.label: List EVERY arrest, citation, and charge to disclose (with dates)
items.detail: Including dismissed, expunged, and out-of-state incidents. USCIS sees your FBI record — non-disclosure is worse than the incident.

items.label: Confirm all tax returns filed for every year as an LPR
items.detail: Filed as a resident (not non-resident). If you owe, set up an IRS payment plan and bring proof.

items.label: Current on child support / alimony (if applicable)
items.detail: Bring proof of payments or your court order.

items.label: Review your PUBLIC social media and be ready to explain it
items.detail: USCIS may review public posts. You don't need to delete your life — just make sure nothing public contradicts your application, and be ready to explain calmly. Set profiles you're unsure about to private.

items.label: Confirm you have never registered to vote or voted as a non-citizen
items.detail: Even accidental voter registration (e.g. offered at the DMV) must be disclosed and explained.

items.label: Create a USCIS online account and complete Form N-400
items.detail: Online filing ($710) is usually faster to track than paper ($760). Fee may change — verify on uscis.gov.

items.label: Pay the fee or apply for a fee waiver/reduction
items.detail: Form I-912 (full waiver) or I-942 (reduced fee) if you qualify by income or hardship.

items.label: Upload supporting evidence and submit
items.detail: Double-check your travel dates and disclosures before submitting — consistency matters.

items.label: Attend your biometrics (fingerprint) appointment
items.detail: USCIS mails the appointment notice. Your fingerprints run an FBI background check — this is why full disclosure matters.

sections.title: Prepare for the English + Civics tests

items.label: Confirm which civics test applies to you
items.detail: The tool derives this from your filing date: 2025 test (128 pool / 20 asked / 12 to pass) for Oct 20 2025+ filers, otherwise the 2008 test (100 / 10 / 6).

items.label: Study the civics questions for your test version
items.detail: Use the official USCIS study materials for the correct test version — the question pools differ.

items.label: Practice the English speaking, reading, and writing tests
items.detail: Read 1 of up to 3 sentences and write 1 of up to 3 correctly; speaking is assessed during the interview.

items.label: Assemble your interview folder
items.detail: Green card, state ID, all passports, travel log, tax transcripts, and certified dispositions for any incident.

items.label: Re-read your N-400 and make sure you can answer consistently
items.detail: Especially trips abroad, addresses, employers, and any 'yes' answers on the moral-character questions.

items.label: Note anything that changed since filing
items.detail: New trips, address or job changes, any new citations — bring documentation.

items.label: Attend the oath ceremony and receive your Naturalization Certificate
items.detail: Keep the certificate extremely safe — replacements (Form N-565) are slow and expensive.

items.label: Apply for a US passport
items.detail: Use your Naturalization Certificate; consider expedited service if you travel soon.

items.label: Update Social Security Administration record
items.detail: Update your citizenship status with the SSA.

items.label: Register to vote and update REAL ID
items.detail: You can now register to vote; update your driver's license/REAL ID status.

items.label: Update your status with your employer (Form I-9)
items.detail: Provide updated work-authorization documents if asked.

items.label: Surrender your Indian passport and apply for OCI
items.detail: India bars dual citizenship — surrender your Indian passport for a Surrender Certificate, then apply for an OCI card. Not legal advice; see our OCI guide.

faq.question: What documents do I need for the citizenship (N-400) interview in 2026?
faq.answer: Bring your green card, a state ID/driver's license, your current and all expired passports, a complete travel history with exact entry/exit dates, IRS tax transcripts for the last 5 years (3 for the spouse track), and certified court dispositions for any arrest or citation ever — even dismissed ones. Spouse-of-citizen applicants also bring the marriage certificate, proof of living together, and the spouse's proof of US citizenship. This tool builds a personalized list based on your answers.

faq.question: What is the new 128-question citizenship test, and does it apply to me?
faq.answer: Applicants who filed Form N-400 on or after October 20, 2025 take the 2025 civics test: a 128-question study pool, 20 questions asked at the interview, and you must answer 12 correctly to pass. If you filed before that date, you take the older 2008 test (100-question pool, 10 asked, 6 to pass). The English test (speaking, reading 1 of 3, writing 1 of 3) is unchanged. Enter your filing date in the tool and it tells you which test and pass bar apply to you. Verify current rules at uscis.gov before relying on this.

faq.question: When can I apply for citizenship — how early?
faq.answer: You can file up to 90 days before your 5-year green-card anniversary (or 3-year anniversary on the spouse-of-citizen track). You must also have at least 30 months of physical presence in the US over those 5 years (18 months over 3 years for spouses), 3 months of residence in your state/USCIS district, and unbroken continuous residence. Enter your 'resident since' date and the tool computes your earliest filing date and counts down to it.

faq.question: Do long trips to India affect my citizenship eligibility?
faq.answer: Yes. A single trip abroad of 6 months or more creates a rebuttable presumption that you broke 'continuous residence', and a trip of 1 year or more breaks it automatically (absent an approved Form N-470). Frequent or long visits to India are a common issue for NRIs, so build a complete, accurate travel log with exact dates before you file — the interview officer will test it against your record.

faq.question: Does becoming a US citizen cancel my Indian citizenship and OCI?
faq.answer: India does not permit dual citizenship, so naturalizing as a US citizen automatically ends your Indian citizenship. You must surrender your Indian passport for a 'Surrender Certificate', after which you can apply for an OCI (Overseas Citizen of India) card that gives lifelong visa-free travel and most rights except voting, certain government jobs, and agricultural land purchase. This is general information, not legal advice — confirm the current process with the Indian consulate/VFS.

faq.question: Should I disclose an old arrest that was dismissed?
faq.answer: Yes — disclose every arrest, citation, or charge, including dismissed and expunged ones, and bring the certified court disposition for each. USCIS runs an FBI fingerprint check and will see your record; failing to disclose looks like a lack of good moral character and is one of the most common causes of denial. The underlying incident is often forgivable; hiding it usually is not.
